g.data-narration: Welcome to Statistical Inference - the art of drawing conclusions about entire populations from sample data.
Statistical inference uses sample data to make generalizations about unknown population parameters through mathematical methods.
g.text: The process of drawing conclusions about an entire
POPULATION based on information from a SAMPLE
Why? Populations are often too large, expensive, or impossible to study entirely.

g.data-narration: A population is the complete set of interest while a sample is its representative subset, denoted by N and n respectively.
g.text: Example: All voters in a country

g.data-narration: Parameters describe populations using Greek letters while statistics describe samples using Roman letters - both serve as numerical summaries.
A good sample must be representative, randomly selected, and adequate in size to yield reliable conclusions.
Simple random sampling gives equal chance to all, stratified divides into groups, systematic picks every kth element, cluster selects whole groups.
Bias is a systematic error causing unrepresentative samples - includes selection, response, non-response, and measurement bias.
g.text: Bias - The Enemy of Good Inference
Bias causes systematic errors making samples unrepresentative, leading to INCORRECT conclusions.

g.data-narration: An unbiased estimator has expected value equal to the true parameter - the sample mean is unbiased for population mean.
g.text: An unbiased statistic has E(statistic) = population parameter
E(x̄) = μ (sample mean estimates population mean)

g.data-narration: The sampling distribution shows how a statistic varies across all possible samples - foundational to understanding inference.
g.text: The probability distribution of a statistic computed from MULTIPLE samples of the same size.

g.data-narration: Estimation uses sample data to guess population parameters - point estimation gives one value, interval estimation gives a range.
g.text: Using sample data to estimate unknown population parameters.

g.data-narration: Hypothesis testing decides whether to accept or reject claims about population parameters using sample evidence and significance levels.
g.text: Deciding whether to accept or reject a claim about a population parameter.

g.data-narration: Statistical inference powers machine learning, software testing, performance analysis, data science, and security in computer science.
g.text: Why Statistical Inference Matters in CS

g.data-narration: Review the key concepts: population vs sample, parameter vs statistic, and the two main branches of inference.
